**Personal names in Malaysia** vary greatly according to ethno-cultural group. [Personal names](/wiki/Personal_name "Personal name") are, to a certain degree, regulated by the national registration department, especially since the introduction of the [National Registration Identity Card (NRIC)](/wiki/Malaysian_identity_card "Malaysian identity card").

[Malays](/wiki/Malays_(ethnic_group) "Malays (ethnic group)"), [Orang Asli](/wiki/Orang_Asli "Orang Asli"), some [Bumiputera](/wiki/Bumiputera_(Malaysia) "Bumiputera (Malaysia)") of Sabah and Sarawak, and [Malaysian Indians](/wiki/Malaysian_Indian "Malaysian Indian") adopt patronymic naming customs. On the other hand, [Malaysian Chinese](/wiki/Malaysian_Chinese "Malaysian Chinese"), some Malays and Bumiputera of Sabah and Sarawak use family names.

## Malay names

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Malaysian_names&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Malay names")]

Traditional Malay names were taken from one of a number of languages, or even a combination of two or more elements from these languages:

* [Malay](/wiki/Malay_language "Malay language") such as Intan, Melati, Kiambang or Tuah
* [Khmer](/wiki/Khmer_language "Khmer language"), [Siamese](/wiki/Thai_language "Thai language") or [Cham](/wiki/Cham_language "Cham language") such as Tam, Som or Lai
* [Javanese](/wiki/Javanese_language "Javanese language") such as Ratnasari, Joyo or Kesuma
* [Sanskrit](/wiki/Sanskrit "Sanskrit") or [Pali](/wiki/Pali "Pali") such as Wira, Darma or Wati

Malay names are not to be confused with Malaysian names. Malay names also exist in various countries other than Malaysia, including among the ethnic Malay communities in [Brunei](/wiki/Brunei "Brunei"), [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia"), [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore "Singapore"), [South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa "South Africa") ([Cape Malays](/wiki/Cape_Malays "Cape Malays")) and [Thailand](/wiki/Thailand "Thailand").

Arabic names were introduced later along with Islamic names but did not become dominant among commoners until the colonial era. Although traditional Malay names were still widely used for centuries afterward, they are now primarily confined to rural areas. Malaysia's National Registration Department doesn't allow names which they deem to have negative or obscene meanings, such as Pendek which means short. The Department additionally bans names with the meaning of colors, animals and natural phenomena. This effectively renders many traditional names illegal including Puteh or Putih (white), Bulan (moon), Suria (sun), Rimau ([tiger](/wiki/Tiger "Tiger")) and Awan (cloud). Because of these restrictions, the vast majority of Malays today tend to favour Arabic names. However, names from the following languages are common as well:

* [Persian](/wiki/Persian_language "Persian language") such as Jihan, Mirza or Shah
* [Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language") or [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") such as Maria, Marina, Johana, and Tiara (from Latin *tiara* "crown, turban")
* [English](/wiki/English_language "English language"), such as Orked (from the English "[orchid](/wiki/Orchid "Orchid")") or Ros (from "rose")

Names of [Arabo](/wiki/Arabic_language "Arabic language")-[Hebrew](/wiki/Hebrew "Hebrew") origins are also common, for example [Adam](/wiki/Adam_in_Islam "Adam in Islam"), [Yaakob](/wiki/Jacob_in_Islam "Jacob in Islam"), [Ishak](/wiki/Isaac_in_Islam "Isaac in Islam"), [Bunyamin](/wiki/Bunyamin "Bunyamin") and [Danial](/wiki/Daniel_in_Islam "Daniel in Islam") and [Sarah](/wiki/Sarah "Sarah"). In addition, names of Arabo-Hebrew origins that are seldom used by Muslim Arabs are widespread among Malays, such as the female names of Saloma and Rohana.

In pre-modern times, words and names of Arabic derivation were adapted to suit the Classical Malay language. This is still reflected in the rural pronunciation of certain Middle Eastern names. Thus, Sharif would be Sarip and Aziz would become Ajis.

### Structure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Malaysian_names&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Structure")]

A Malay's name consists of a personal name, which is used to address them in all circumstances, almost always followed by a patronym. Thus, most Malays do not use family names or surnames. In this respect, Malay names are similar to [Icelandic naming conventions](/wiki/Icelandic_naming_conventions "Icelandic naming conventions"). For men, the patronym consists of the title *bin* (from the Arabic بن, meaning 'son of') followed by his father's personal name. If Osman has a son called Musa, Musa will be known as Musa bin Osman. For women, the patronym consists of the title *binti* (from the Arabic بنت, meaning 'daughter of') followed by her father's name. Thus, if Musa has a daughter called Aisyah, Aisyah will be known as Aisyah binti Musa. Upon marriage, a woman does not change her name, as is done in many cultures.

In the past it was uncommon for a Malay to have more than one personal name, but in modern times Malay names may consist of two and sometimes three personal names.

As of January 2022, the longest recorded name for a Malaysian is "Princess Aura Nurr Ermily Amara Auliya Bidadari Nawal El-Zendra", comprising 63 letters including spaces.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

Some are taken from public figures around the world, such as Mohammad Rifae Zidane, whose third personal name is taken from the [famous footballer](/wiki/Zinedine_Zidane "Zinedine Zidane"). Some people have names from other languages that have a different Malay meaning. For example, the daughter of actor [Scha Alyahya](/wiki/Scha_Alyahya "Scha Alyahya") and [Awal Ashaari](/wiki/Awal_Ashaari "Awal Ashaari"), named Lara Alana, faced criticism from Malaysians because "Lara" means "painful" in Malay, although it means "protector" in [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin").[[2]](#cite_note-2)

### Patronymic

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Malaysian_names&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Patronymic")]

The patronymic is employed by almost all Malays in accordance with local customs as well as ones adopted from the Arabs, historically Jews and others. Sometimes the title part of the patronymic, *Bin* or *Binti*, is reduced to *B.* for men, or to *Bt.*, *Bte.* or *Bint.* for women. Foreigners sometimes take this abbreviation erroneously for a [middle initial](/wiki/Middle_name "Middle name"). In general practice, most Malays omit the title *Bin* or *Binti* from their names. Therefore, the two examples from the paragraph above would be known as Musa Osman and Aisyah Musa. When presented in this way, the second part of the name is often mistaken by foreigners for a [family name](/wiki/Family_name "Family name").

When someone is referred to using only one name, the first name is always used, never the second (because it would be inappropriate and rude to call someone by their father's name). Thus, Musa Osman is Mr Musa (or Encik Musa in Malay), and Aisyah Musa is Mrs/Ms/Miss Aisyah (or Puan/Cik Aisyah in Malay). Occasionally, however, a man's personal name comes after the Islamic prophet Mohammed's name, or the word [Abdul](/wiki/Abdul "Abdul"). In such a case, the man will usually be referred to by his second name, if the third name is the patronymic. For example, Mohammed Hisyam bin Ariffin would be referred by the name Mr Hisyam, or Abdul Rahman bin Rasyid would be referred to as Mr Abdul Rahman.

It is argued[*[by whom?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Words_to_watch#Unsupported_attributions "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Words to watch")*] that the Mr or Mrs form of address is not compatible with the Malay naming system, probably due to the lack of family or surnames.[*[according to whom?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Words_to_watch#Unsupported_attributions "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Words to watch")*] It is therefore customary to address Malays using the Malay forms of address (Encik or Puan/Cik).

### Honorifics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Malaysian_names&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Honorifics")]

In olden times, the [first group of Chinese people in Malaysia](/wiki/Peranakan_Chinese "Peranakan Chinese") used to be held in high regard by Malays. Some Malays in the past may have taken the word "Baba", referring to Chinese males, and put it into their name, when this used to be the case.[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5) This is not followed by the younger generation,[*[who?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Words_to_watch#Unsupported_attributions "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Words to watch")*] and the current [Chinese Malaysians](/wiki/Chinese_Malaysians "Chinese Malaysians") do not have the same social status as they previously had.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Nicknames

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Malaysian_names&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Nicknames")]

Malays of the Peninsular have a unique convention of nicknames based on the order of their birth:[[6]](#cite_note-6)

* the eldest born (*sulung*) is *Pak*/*Mak Long*
* the second (*tengah*, 'middle') is *Pak*/*Mak Ngah*
* third (*alang*, 'middling'), is *Pak*/*Mak Lang*
* fourth (*panjang*, 'long') is *Pak Panjang*/*Mak Anjang*
* fifth (*pandak*, 'short') is *Pak*/*Mak Andak*
* sixth (*muda*, 'young') is *Pak*/*Mak Uda*
* the youngest (*bongsu*) is *Pak*/*Mak Su*.

### Second personal names or double names

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Malaysian_names&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Second personal names or double names")]

Another feature in Malay names, which is very common, is the existence of second personal names or double names. This seems to have been developed in response to the use of very popular Muslim names, like [Muhammad](/wiki/Muhammad_(name) "Muhammad (name)") and Ahmad for men, and Nur and Siti for women. Bearers of these names, and their variants, often add a more distinctive second name, like Muhammad Osman or Nur Mawar. The patronym is then added after these.

The popular first elements in double Malay male names are:

* [Muhammad](/wiki/Muhammad_(name) "Muhammad (name)")/Mohammad/Mohammed (often abbreviated to Muhd., Mohd., Md. or simply M.)
* Mat – the Malay variant of Muhammad. Mat is also the casual spoken form of names ending with -mad or -mat such as Ahmad, Rahmat, Samad, etc.
* Mamat - another variety of Muhammad
* Ahmad
* Awang (Commonly used in Kelantan, Terengganu, Sarawak, Brunei and Kutai)

The most common first elements in double Malay female names are:[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

* Nur/Nurul/Noor/Nor
* [Siti](/wiki/Siti_(name) "Siti (name)")/Ct
* Dayang (Commonly used in Brunei, Kutai and Sarawak)

A special case of double names for men is the use of [Abdul](/wiki/Abdul "Abdul"). Following Arabic naming practices, *Abdul* simply means 'servant of' and must be followed by one of the [names of God in the Qur'an](/wiki/Names_of_God_in_the_Qur%27an "Names of God in the Qur'an"); for example Abdul Haqq means 'servant of the Truth'.

Thus, Osman may have another son called Abdul Haqq, who is known as Abdul Haqq bin Osman, or Abdul Haqq Osman. Then he, in turn, may have a daughter called Nur Mawar, who is known as Nur Mawar binti Abdul Haqq, or Nor Mawar Abdul Haqq. It is often common to drop the first element in these double names, even if it is Abdul, and so the examples could be known as Haqq Osman and Mawar Haqq.

### Malay titles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Malaysian_names&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Malay titles")]

Main article: [Malay styles and titles](/wiki/Malay_styles_and_titles "Malay styles and titles")

### Hereditary titles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Malaysian_names&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Hereditary titles")]

In different parts of, but not exclusive to, Malaysia, traditionally inherited (patrilineally) [Malay titles](/wiki/Malay_titles "Malay titles") and sometimes matrilineally, are used and often incorporated into the naming system as the first part of double names. Most of those with these titles are descended from royalty or nobility.

The examples of inherited titles are:

by Patrilineal Royal descent (Malay)

* Tunku (male and female)
* Tengku (male and female)
* Raja (male and female)

by Patrilineal Royal descent (Malay-Mon-Khmer)

* Yang (male and female)
* Long (male and female)

by Patrilineal Royal descent (Acheh-Malay)

* Teuku (male)
* Cut (female)

by Patrilineal Royal descent ([Buginese-Malay](/wiki/Bugis-Malay "Bugis-Malay") and [Makassarese](/wiki/Makassar_people "Makassar people")-[Malay](/wiki/Malay_race "Malay race"))

* [Daeng](/wiki/Malay_styles_and_titles#Other_Malay_titles_by_inheritance "Malay styles and titles") (male and female)
* [Andi](/wiki/Malay_styles_and_titles#Other_Malay_titles_by_inheritance "Malay styles and titles") (male and female)
* [Tok](/wiki/Malay_styles_and_titles#Other_Malay_titles_by_inheritance "Malay styles and titles") (male and female)
* [Raja](/wiki/Malay_styles_and_titles#Other_Malay_titles_by_inheritance "Malay styles and titles") (male and female)
* [Tengku](/wiki/Malay_styles_and_titles#Other_Malay_titles_by_inheritance "Malay styles and titles") (male and female)
* [Pangiran](/wiki/Pangiran "Pangiran") (male and female)

by Patrilineal Royal descent (Malay)

* Engku (male and female)
* Ungku (male and female)
* Tunku (male and female)
* Ku (male and female)

by Patrilineal and Matrilineal Noble descent ([Arabic](/wiki/Arabic_people "Arabic people")-[Malay](/wiki/Malay_race "Malay race"))

* [Syed](/wiki/Sayyid "Sayyid")/Sharifah (for male and female, respectively) — indicating direct patrilineal descent from the family of [Muhammad](/wiki/Prophet_Muhammad "Prophet Muhammad").
* Mior (for male only) — indicating direct matrilineal descent from the family of [Muhammad](/wiki/Prophet_Muhammad "Prophet Muhammad").
* Awangku/Pangiran Muda/Pangiran Anak/Pengiran, Dayangku (commonly found in Brunei and [Sarawak](/wiki/Sarawak "Sarawak"), for male and female, denoting [Bruneian](/wiki/Brunei "Brunei")-Malay aristocratic descent).
* Tuan (used more generally as a respectful term of address for men, like 'sir')
* Awang/Dayang, Abang/Dayang or Dayangku (used in Brunei, Kutai and [Sarawak](/wiki/Sarawak "Sarawak"), for male and female, respectively) [[7]](#cite_note-7)

by Patrilineal Noble descent ([Thai-Malay](/wiki/Thai_Malays "Thai Malays") and [Javanese](/wiki/Javanese_language "Javanese language")-[Malay](/wiki/Malay_race "Malay race"))

* Wan (male and female)
* Megat (male)
* Puteri (female)
* Nik (male and female)
* Che (male and female)
* Tun (male and female)
* Raden (male and female)

All hereditary titles are controlled and regulated as well as registered by the Malaysian National Registration Department and must appear in the National Registration Identity Cards (NRIC), passports as well as all official documents. A person may not in any circumstances be denied or stripped of his or her hereditary titles and persons with no evidence of inheritance are not allowed to carry these titles in accordance to local customs as well as the national registration naming regulations.

### Non-hereditary titles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Malaysian_names&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Non-hereditary titles")]

The titles above should not be confused with those given by special award which are non-hereditary, like 'Datuk', 'Tan Sri' and 'Tun'.

These titles are usually awarded by the [Sultans](/wiki/Sultan "Sultan") of the recipients' respective states as well as the [Yang Dipertuan Agong](/wiki/Yang_Dipertuan_Agong "Yang Dipertuan Agong") and the state [Yang Dipertua](/w/index.php?title=Yang_Dipertua&action=edit&redlink=1 "Yang Dipertua (page does not exist)") as recognition for their contributions and services to the nation and the respective states. For example, the title 'Datuk' is given to Malaysians of all races as an honorary title. An example is [Datuk Lee Chong Wei](/wiki/Lee_Chong_Wei "Lee Chong Wei"), a famous [badminton](/wiki/Badminton "Badminton") player who was awarded the title as recognition to his achievement in becoming the third Malaysian to win a silver Olympic medal [2008 Beijing Summer Olympics](/wiki/2008_Summer_Olympics "2008 Summer Olympics"). If the recipient is a man, his wife is automatically bestowed with the title 'Datin' but not in reverse.

The title 'Tun' is reserved for nationally important persons, like the fourth [Prime Minister of Malaysia](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Malaysia "Prime Minister of Malaysia") [Tun Mahathir bin Mohamad](/wiki/Mahathir_Mohamad "Mahathir Mohamad"). He was given the title after his resignation at 2003.

For an example of a complex name, one former [Prime Minister of Malaysia](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Malaysia "Prime Minister of Malaysia") has the full name [Dato' Seri Mohd Najib bin Tun Haji Abdul Razak](/wiki/Najib_Razak "Najib Razak"), where 'Dato' Seri' is a [Malay title](/wiki/Malay_title "Malay title") of honour, 'Mohd Najib' is his personal name (often further abbreviated to 'Najib'), 'bin' introduces his father's titles and names, *Tun* is a higher honour, 'Haji' denotes his father as a pilgrim to [Mecca](/wiki/Mecca "Mecca"), and 'Abdul Razak' is his father's personal name (often abbreviated to *Razak*). The entire name has various shorter forms, like 'Mohd Najib Tun Abdul Razak', 'Najib Tun Razak' and 'Najib Razak').

### Haji or Hajjah

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Malaysian_names&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Haji or Hajjah")]

If someone has been on the [Hajj](/wiki/Hajj "Hajj"), the [pilgrimage](/wiki/Pilgrimage "Pilgrimage") to [Mecca](/wiki/Mecca "Mecca"), they may be called [Haji](/wiki/Hajji "Hajji") for men or Hajjah for women. Thus, if Musa Bin Osman went on the Hajj, he could be called Haji Musa Bin Osman, and his daughter Aisyah might be called Aisyah Binti Haji Musa. If Aisyah herself had completed the hajj, her name would be Hajjah Aisyah Binti Haji Musa. The titles can also be shortened in writing to 'Hj.' for Haji and 'Hjh' for Hajjah.

## Chinese names

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Malaysian_names&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Chinese names")]

Traditional [Chinese names](/wiki/Chinese_name "Chinese name") 姓名 are used among the [Malaysian Chinese](/wiki/Malaysian_Chinese "Malaysian Chinese"). These names are usually represented as three words, for example *Chua Hock Beng* (for a male) or *Foo Li Leen* (for a female). The first word 'Chua' and 'Foo' are the [Chinese surnames](/wiki/Chinese_surnames "Chinese surnames") 姓氏, which are passed down from a father to all of his children. The two other parts of the name form an indivisible [Chinese given name](/wiki/Chinese_given_name "Chinese given name") 名字, which may contain a [generation name](/wiki/Generation_name "Generation name") 字辈. Among some Chinese, their names can also be represented as four words, for example *Au Yong Chee Keong*, in which the first two words 'Au Yong' is the surname followed by the given name of 'Chee Keong'. Back in the old days, Chinese names can also be represented as two words, for example *Teoh Eng*, in which the first word 'Teoh' is the surname followed by the given name of 'Eng'. In other cultures, the family name is sometimes shifted to the end of the name (for example, *Li Leen Foo*).

Some Chinese use a Western personal name (for example, *Denise Lau*), and some use this in preference to a Chinese given name. Most of these are used by Chinese [Malaysian Christians](/wiki/Christianity_in_Malaysia "Christianity in Malaysia"), or Chinese who primarily speak English. On official documents, it's *Western given name - Chinese given name - surname* (e.g., Denise Sook Ying Lau). In general practice, only one of the given names (the Western or Chinese name) is used. Chinese Malaysian Muslims may use Arabic given names while some use Arabic-derived Chinese names, e.g., Firdaus Fong Siew Chong.

As no formal system of [romanisation](/wiki/Romanisation "Romanisation") is imposed on Chinese names in Malaysia at the time of birth registration, names are often romanised according to the judgment of the registration clerk or according to the preference of the proposer. Hence, romanisation errors are not uncommon resulting in unusual names. Since the 1980s, [Pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin "Pinyin") names are becoming more common, although one would not say popular. The Pinyin form is based on [Mandarin (Putonghua)](/wiki/Standard_Chinese "Standard Chinese"), whereas most existing romanised surnames are based on [dialects](/wiki/Varieties_of_Chinese "Varieties of Chinese"). For example, Tan (in [Fujian](/wiki/Hokkien "Hokkien") dialect) is [Chen](/wiki/Chen_(surname) "Chen (surname)") in the Pinyin form. In [Foochow](/wiki/Foochow "Foochow") and [Henghua](/wiki/Henghua "Henghua") dialects, the existing romanised form is Ding (or Ting). Both Tan and Ding (or Ting) are correct depending on which sound they prefer as the same Chinese character have more than one pronunciation. For example, in [Hakka](/wiki/Hakka "Hakka") dialect, the existing romanised form for [Chen](/wiki/Chen_(surname) "Chen (surname)") is Chin, whereas in [Cantonese](/wiki/Cantonese "Cantonese") and Kwongsai dialects, the existing romanised form is pronounced as Chan. Meanwhile, in [Teochew](/wiki/Teochew_Min "Teochew Min") dialect, the existing romanised form for [Chen](/wiki/Chen_(surname) "Chen (surname)") is pronounced as Tang.

As parents prefer their children to have the same romanised surname as their father, names such as Tan Jia Ling where Tan is in [Hokkien](/wiki/Hokkien_dialect "Hokkien dialect") and the given name of Jia Ling in Mandarin are becoming a common form of naming style these days.

## Indian names

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Malaysian_names&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Indian names")]

Officially, [Malaysian Indians](/wiki/Malaysian_Indian "Malaysian Indian") use a patronymic naming system combining their traditional [Indian names](/wiki/Indian_name "Indian name") with some Malay words,[[8]](#cite_note-8) while others use [Tamil](/wiki/Tamil_language "Tamil language"), [Telugu](/wiki/Telugu_language "Telugu language"), [Malayalam](/wiki/Malayalam "Malayalam"), or [Sanskrit](/wiki/Sanskrit "Sanskrit") names.[[9]](#cite_note-9) A man's name would consist of his personal name followed by the Malay phrase *anak lelaki*, meaning 'son of', and then his father's name. A woman's name would consist of her personal name followed by the Malay phrase *anak perempuan*, meaning 'daughter of', and then her father's name. The Malay patronymic phrase is often abbreviated to *a/l* ('son of') or *a/p* ('daughter of') and then their father's name. In many circumstances, the intervening Malay is omitted, and the father's name follows immediately after a person's given name.

Following traditional practice from South India, the father's name is sometimes abbreviated to an [initial](/wiki/Initialism "Initialism") and placed before the personal name. Thus, a man called Anbuselvan whose father is called Ramanan may be called Anbuselvan anak lelaki Ramanan (formal), Anbuselvan a/l Ramanan (as on his government [identification card](/wiki/Malaysian_identity_card "Malaysian identity card")), Anbuselvan Ramanan or R. Anbuselvan. Whereas, his daughter Mathuram would be called Mathuram anak perempuan Anbuselvan (formal), Mathuram a/p Anbuselvan (as on her government identification card), Mathuram Anbuselvan or A. Mathuram. Although not recorded officially, a South Indian woman may use her husband's personal name instead of her father's name after marriage. For example, Letchumy a/p Subramaniam who married to Kumar a/l Ganesan may identify herself as Letchumy Kumar or simply Mrs Kumar.

Indian Malaysian Muslims, like ethnic Malays, use Arabic names or names of their own languages such as Mohd Hasaan Kutty, and Noor Faridah Begum Merican. Meanwhile, Christian names with a touch of Indian influence and own family name or father's name such as David Vijay Panicker, and Elizabeth Shanti Arumugam may also be used by Indian Malaysian Christians.

Sikh Malaysian usage follows either the western fashion of personal names followed by the family name or more commonly the South Indian pattern with the personal name followed with the phrase "anak lelaki" (son of) for males and "anak perempuan (daughter of) for females. Examples of Sikh western-style usage where their full personal names followed by their family (or clan) names are: Harjit Singh Gill (for a male) or Asha Kaur Dhillon (for a female). Examples of usage in the South Indian fashion would be: Harjit Singh a/l Balwinder Singh (for a male) and Asha Kaur a/p Jagdeep Singh (for a female).

## Names of members of other groups

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Malaysian_names&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Names of members of other groups")]

[Peninsular](/wiki/Malay_Peninsula "Malay Peninsula") [Orang Asli](/wiki/Orang_Asli "Orang Asli") and [Sarawakian](/wiki/Sarawak "Sarawak") [Bumiputra](/wiki/Bumiputra "Bumiputra") use the Malay word *anak* ('child of') to form their patronymics regardless of an individual's sex, for example, *Sagong anak Tasi* and *Narang anak Meringgai*. However, most of the new generation indigenous people in Sabah and Sarawak who live in town areas and who practice Christianity as a religion, tend to have a Christian first name, followed by ethnic name, surname or father's name. For example, John anak Gani (Gani being the father's name), Melissa Melanie Rawing (Rawing being a surname) and Thomas Gimbang Kitingan (Gimbang being an ethnic name and Kitingan being a surname).

Some [Sabah](/wiki/Sabah "Sabah") and [Sarawak](/wiki/Sarawak "Sarawak") Bumiputra have patronymics in the same fashion as Malays, using *bin* or *binti*, while others have [patrilineal surnames](/wiki/Patrilineal_surname "Patrilineal surname") which are handed down unchanged from generation to generation.

[Minangkabau descendants](/wiki/Minangkabau_people "Minangkabau people") use clan and tribal names passed down matrilineally.

[Kristang people](/wiki/Kristang_people "Kristang people") usually have [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_names "Portuguese names"), or, at least, more European-sounding names, including inherited [family names](/wiki/Family_name "Family name") such as Joseph Gabriel Monterio, Christina Alice Fernandez, and so on. In fact, Arabs, Persians, and Portuguese have common denominator in influence in names: Fatima, Omar, and Soraya. These names are common in Portugal given by Arab, and Persian influences.
